title: Pixel Game
description: Build a tiny scene that comes alive when you press the arrow buttons.
intro:
Build your own pixel-game scene that animates when you press the arrow keys. You'll fill in a few small Python functions, run them together, and end up with a tiny game you can save and take home.

How to work through it:

  1. Read the first Pixel coordinates card below so you know how the canvas grid works.
  2. Pick a task card on the left, then type your code into the editor on its right.
  3. Click ▶ Run Project (in the sidebar) to see your scene on the canvas.
  4. Watch for the green ✓ tick on each task as you finish it.
  5. Once the player can move, click ⛶ on the canvas to play in full screen (press Esc to exit).
  6. Use the 💾 Save button (or Ctrl+S) to save your file so you can keep working on it next class.
  7. Next class, click 📂 Open at the top to load your saved file and pick up where you left off.

A few useful things to know:

  • Tasks marked optional are stretch goals, finish the others first.
  • The arrow keys on your keyboard work too (when you're not editing code).
  • Anything you print() shows up under the canvas, great for showing a score.

💻 This project works best on a laptop. You'll be using the keyboard a lot.
editablePreamble:
state.player_x = 10
state.player_y = 10
# Add your own state variables below, e.g.
# state.score = 0
# state.colour = 'red'

tasks.title: Pixel coordinates
tasks.content:
Use the draw(x, y, colour) function to colour in cells on the canvas.

Every cell has an address written as (x, y):

  • x goes across (0 on the left, 19 on the right).
  • y goes up (0 at the bottom, 19 at the top).
draw(1, 0, 'red')→
y2(0, 2)(1, 2)(2, 2)
1(0, 1)(1, 1)(2, 1)
0(0, 0)(1, 0)(2, 0)
012
x

Colour is a string (in quotes). Try 'red', 'blue', 'gold', 'hotpink', 'tomato', or 'aquamarine'. See the full list of colour names.
tasks.footer: x across, y up. (0, 0) is bottom-left.

tasks.title: Colour the corners
tasks.areaDescription: Called from draw_scene. Paint the four corner cells of the canvas.
tasks.guidance:
Warm-up: draw a coloured cell in each of the four corners of the canvas.

The corners are at (0, 0), (19, 0), (0, 19), and (19, 19). Use draw(x, y, 'colour'), pick whatever colours you like.
tasks.starterBody:
draw(0, 0, 'red')
# add the other three corners

tasks.title: Draw a border
tasks.areaDescription: Called from draw_scene. Paint the 76 edge cells of the canvas.
tasks.guidance:
A border goes all the way around the edge of the canvas. There are 76 cells in the border, way too many to type one by one. Use for loops instead.

Tip: you'll need four loops: bottom row, top row, left column, right column. Or one loop with some clever maths!

tasks.title: Draw the background
tasks.areaDescription: Called from draw_scene first, so the player sits on top. Free creative space.
tasks.guidance:
Time to make the canvas your own. draw_background() is your blank slate: whatever you draw here sits behind the player.

Start by calling the two functions you've already written (draw_border() and draw_corners()), then add anything else you can think of. A few ideas to get you started:

  • A row of coloured rectangles along the bottom for grass or sand
  • Rainbow stripes across the middle
  • A pattern of dots
  • A shape using nested loops, like a triangle, diamond, or letter
  • Anything else you can imagine

Tick the box on this card when you're happy with your background.
tasks.starterBody:
draw_border()
draw_corners()
# add your own stuff! rectangles, stripes, shapes, anything.

tasks.title: Draw the player
tasks.areaDescription: Called from draw_scene after draw_background, so the player sits on top.
tasks.guidance:
The player lives at state.player_x, state.player_y. The State section starts these at (10, 10).

The starter copies those into local x and y so you can tweak them, then draws a single lime cell. Want a bigger character? Add more draw calls for cells around the centre, like draw(x + 1, y, 'lime') or draw(x, y + 1, 'red').
tasks.starterBody:
x = state.player_x
y = state.player_y
draw(x, y, 'lime')

tasks.title: Build the scene
tasks.areaDescription: Runs once on Run, and again after every arrow press. Call your other functions in order.
tasks.guidance:
This function runs every time you press a button. Its job is to put the whole picture together by calling your other functions.

Order matters: later draws appear on top of earlier ones. Background first, player last.

tasks.title: Updating variables and state
tasks.content:
A variable can be updated by reading its current value, doing some maths, and storing the new value back. The pattern is x = x + 1 (read x, add 1, store the new x). It works the same way with state attributes.

Before
state.player_x  is  10
Run this code
state.player_x = state.player_x - 1
After
state.player_x  is  9

This is how your key handlers will move the player: read where they are, change it, store the new position back.

Python has a shorthand for this pattern: state.player_x -= 1 means the same thing as state.player_x = state.player_x - 1. Either works.
tasks.footer: Read it, change it, store it back.

tasks.title: Make the buttons move the player
tasks.areaDescription: Each handler runs when its arrow button is clicked. draw_scene re-runs automatically afterwards, so the player visibly moves.
tasks.guidance:
The four arrow buttons each call one of these handler functions. on_left_key is already filled in for you, with state.player_x = state.player_x - 1 (subtract one from the player's x position).

Fill in the other three so each button moves the player one cell:

  • on_right_key: add one to state.player_x
  • on_up_key: add one to state.player_y (up means a bigger y)
  • on_down_key: subtract one from state.player_y

The def lines are locked, so you can't accidentally rename them. Delete the pass line in each empty function before adding your code, otherwise it will stay there as a leftover (it still works, but it's tidier without it).

tasks.title: Stop the player at the walls
tasks.guidance:
Right now the player can walk off the edge of the canvas. Fix that.

In each of the four key handlers (above), check the player's position before moving. If they're already at the edge, don't move them. For example, in on_left_key, only do state.player_x -= 1 if state.player_x > 0.

The canvas runs from 0 to 19 in both x and y.

Tick this card when the player can't walk off any edge.

tasks.title: Add a score
tasks.guidance:
Add a state.score = 0 line to the State section. In one or more of your key handlers, bump the score (state.score += 1). In draw_scene, print('Score:', state.score) to show it under the canvas.

Variations: score only when you hit a target cell, score that resets when you press a particular key, score that grows a bar of cells. Up to you.

Tick this card when your score does something visible.

tasks.title: How a key press works
tasks.content:
You might be wondering: I only wrote state.player_x = state.player_x - 1 in on_left_key, so how does the player actually move on the canvas? Here's the loop the project runs whenever you press an arrow:

1. You press ←
(or click the arrow button)
→
2. The project runs on_left_key()
your code changes state
→
3. The project runs draw_scene() again
the project's job, automatic
→
4. The canvas redraws
You see the player moved

The trick is step 3: the project runs draw_scene again all by itself after every key press. Your handler only had to change state.player_x. draw_player then reads the new value and draws the player at its new spot.

This is why each of your handlers can be just one line: you don't have to draw anything yourself, you just update where the player is. The project takes care of showing the change.
tasks.footer: You change state. The project redraws.

tasks.title: Invent something
tasks.areaDescription: Free coding space. Code here runs at the top level after Setup. Add helpers, extra state, new functions, anything.
tasks.guidance:
Your turn. The editor on the right is a blank slate at the top level of your project. You can:

  • Define a new helper function (e.g. def draw_creature(x, y):) and call it from draw_scene
  • Add a new state variable and use it in your handlers
  • Compute something fancy using imports (import random, import math)
  • Anything else you want to try

Tick this card when you've built something you're proud of.
tasks.starterBody:
# Your code here. Whatever you write runs at the top level
# after Setup, before the function definitions.

tasks.title: Extension ideas
tasks.content:
Done with the core tasks? Here are some ways to take the project further. All of these go in the Invent something editor above.

📜 How the project knows when to run your code

The project understands a small set of special function names. If you define a function with one of these names, the project will call it for you at the right time. Other names (like draw_player or bunny_jump) won't run automatically; you have to call them yourself, usually from inside draw_scene.

NameWhen the project calls it
draw_scene()once at start, and again after every key press
on_left_key()when ← is pressed
on_right_key()when → is pressed
on_up_key()when ↑ is pressed
on_down_key()when ↓ is pressed
on_key(key)when any letter or number key is pressed (the key is passed in)

That last one, on_key, is what unlocks the multiplayer extension below.

🎮 Multiplayer with WASD

Define on_key(key) and use if / elif to decide what each key does. Each press passes in the key as a string, so you can read 'w', 'a', 's', or 'd':

state.p2_x = 5
state.p2_y = 5

def draw_player2():
    draw(state.p2_x, state.p2_y, 'magenta')

def on_key(key):
    if key == 'w':
        state.p2_y = state.p2_y + 1
    elif key == 's':
        state.p2_y = state.p2_y - 1
    elif key == 'a':
        state.p2_x = state.p2_x - 1
    elif key == 'd':
        state.p2_x = state.p2_x + 1

Then call draw_player2() from draw_scene. Two players, two sets of keys, one screen.

🎲 Random colours and positions

Python's random module picks random things for you. It isn't one of the special names above, so you call it yourself from inside your own code:

import random

# A random colour from a list
colour = random.choice(['red', 'blue', 'gold', 'hotpink', 'lime'])

# A random cell on the canvas
x = random.randint(0, 19)
y = random.randint(0, 19)
draw(x, y, colour)

Use it to scatter dots in your background, spawn random targets, or pick a random colour for the player every time they move.

💡 Other ideas

  • A target: draw a target cell at a random position. When the player lands on it, bump the score and move the target.
  • A trail: save every cell the player has visited in a list, draw them all in draw_background.
  • Levels: count moves in state.moves. Every 20 moves, change the background colour.
  • Enemies: draw a second character at a fixed spot. End the game (print('Game over')) if the player lands on it.
tasks.footer: Pick one, or invent your own.
